title: George Forster's dazzlingly humane vision
gist: Reviewing Andrea Wulf's biography 'The Traveller,' Mathew Lyons traces how naturalist George Forster's 1772-75 circumnavigation with Cook shattered his inherited Eurocentric worldview, leading him to reject Enlightenment racial-classification schemes (Linnaeus, Kant, Rousseau) as 'meaningless drivel' and defend cultural relativism, before his radicalisation in — and disillusionment with — the French Revolution.

insights.claim: On Cook's second circumnavigation (1772-75), Forster arrived confident of 'the superiority of a state of civilisation over that of barbarism' but within the voyage reversed himself, writing that European contact had been 'wholly disadvantageous to the nations of the South Seas.'
insights.speaker: Mathew Lyons
insights.so_what: A documented case of direct field observation overturning an inherited ideological prior within a single multi-year voyage — a useful illustration of belief revision under first-hand evidence.
insights.quote: our intercourse has been wholly disadvantageous to the nations of the South Seas
insights.locator: When he first encounters indigenous peoples

insights.claim: Forster explicitly rejected the racial-classification systems of his Enlightenment contemporaries — Linnaeus' four colour-coded human subspecies and Kant's claim that 'red' Americans were 'incapable of any culture' and that 'All Negroes stink' — dismissing such 'nomenclature' as meaningless.
insights.speaker: Mathew Lyons
insights.so_what: Positions Forster as an early, explicit dissenter from the racial-taxonomy consensus of major Enlightenment thinkers — relevant context for histories of scientific racism.
insights.locator: Forster turned against these fathers, too

insights.claim: Forster argued that 'virtues and vice are relative concepts' and that no one should judge a people without considering the moral principles guiding them, going so far as to defend cannibalism as 'neither unnatural nor criminal in itself' while condemning his shipmates' instinct toward summary lethal justice.
insights.speaker: Mathew Lyons
insights.so_what: An early articulation of cultural-relativist ethics applied specifically against colonial violence, framed by the reviewer as radical for its time.
insights.quote: Virtues and vice are relative concepts [and] no-one should judge a people without considering the moral principles that guide them

insights.claim: Forster's father Reinhold exemplified the opposing, unreformed Eurocentric view: he described Pacific islanders on Tanna as bordering 'nearest upon the tribe of monkeys' and shot at an islander over a price dispute, saying he would fight 'to the last drop of my blood in defence of my property.'
insights.speaker: Mathew Lyons
insights.so_what: Provides the direct ideological foil that sharpens the contrast with George Forster's relativism, illustrating the range of Enlightenment-era colonial attitudes within a single expedition.
insights.quote: border the nearest upon the tribe of monkeys
insights.locator: On Tanna, in the Vanuatu archipelago

insights.claim: Forster embraced the French Revolution's violence, rejoicing in 1789 that 'violence... not the gentle persuasion of reason' had toppled the government in Liège, and by 1792 was denouncing monarchs as 'the scum of the human race' while serving as vice president of Mainz's revolutionary provisional government.
insights.speaker: Mathew Lyons
insights.so_what: Documents Forster's arc from Enlightenment naturalist to revolutionary political actor — a biographical data point on how Pacific-voyage relativism translated into domestic radical politics.
insights.quote: the scum of the human race, these degenerate, imbecile, privileged people

insights.claim: After witnessing the Revolution firsthand as part of Mainz's delegation to Paris in 1793, Forster grew disillusioned, writing 'There is no virtue in the revolution... It disgusts me,' and died in ill health months later in January 1794, aged 39.
insights.speaker: Mathew Lyons
insights.so_what: Marks the collapse of Forster's revolutionary idealism upon direct exposure to Parisian reality — a recurring pattern among Enlightenment sympathizers of the Terror era.
insights.quote: There is no virtue in the revolution... It disgusts me.

insights.claim: Forster's capacity for forgiveness extended to tolerating his wife Therese's affairs, including one with Wilhelm Meyer before their marriage and a later long-term arrangement with Ferdinand Huber, who fathered two of Therese's children while living with the couple.
insights.speaker: Mathew Lyons
insights.so_what: Illustrates the same relativist, non-judgmental ethic Forster applied to other cultures extended into his own private life — a biographical throughline the reviewer highlights as near-inhuman forbearance.
insights.quote: I imagined an equal sharing… with no lessening of my own rights
insights.locator: Forster took the pain and humiliation in his stride
